sentences.text: Yes, of course there were, but relatively few, and they must have moved out.
I don't know precisely details about that, but they must have moved out.
But as I said, that area would have been some-- a large part of that area must have been already before the war something like maybe almost 100% Jewish, large parts of it.
It depends where because certain areas like what I said, Nalewki and the northern parts of it were almost only Jewish, I think.
But the other streets, like in the sm-- what was called small ghetto, they were-- it was mixed.
I think that was probably something like there might have been quite a large Polish population in some areas.
But the majority was-- was Jewish even then.
No, no, no.
They had to leave.
The ghetto was-- was-- [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish-- Jewish living area.
It was-- it was absolutely only Jews were living there.
There were no Poles living in the ghetto.
There was a church in the ghetto, or maybe more than one, but I remember that there was one in Leszno, which is actually one of the main streets.
And there was the church which was, I think, all the time of the ghetto was being used because there were a number of converted Jews-- Jews who converted to Christianity before the war.
And because the Germans were treating the Jews on their-- according to their racial background and not religious one, they were-- they had to move into ghetto even if they were Christian.
And I remember that pl-- that church because it had a garden next to it, and I-- the garden was open.
And I-- and gardens were almost nonexistent in the ghetto.
And I used later on in 1941 and in 1942 when I sometimes went and wanted to have a quiet hour, I would go with a book and sit in the garden there and read.
And whoever heard-- and there-- there existed-- there existed a serum against typhoid, which according to what I was told was a serum invented by Dr.
Weigl in Lwow in Poland.
But that serum was absolutely rare and very difficult to get and terribly expensive.
But of course whoever managed to have the money together, then they would-- they would get immunized.
And I think my parents were immunized, but I think I was not immunized.
I think the serum was not considered safe for children.
I was not immunized, but I-- but there was this constant fear in those times that one would get infected.

And my mother was-- who was absolutely crazy about hygiene, would always tell when I came home in those days, she would tell me to undress at the entrance and check all my clothes that one didn't find any lice because lice were the carriers of the-- of the typhoid.
And somehow, she-- we-- we managed to stay OK.
Somehow we managed to avoid the disease.
But that winter in '40, '41, many, many people died from typhoid.
That was the-- that's [NON-ENGLISH].
How to say?
I don't know.
It's not a-- it's not a typhoid, an intestinal typhoid, but it's a typhoid causing spots.
Anyway, and-- Did they have any hospital or doctors, nurses, anything?
Oh, but were-- there were doctors and nurses, yes.
And We were lucky, again, that one of my-- an-- an uncle, husband of my youngest aunt was a doctor, was a surgeon.
And because of that, we probably were able still to get some serum and so on.
But there were doctors, yes, but as to the conditions in the hospitals in ghetto, I really don't know because I haven't ever been in.
But all of those things which belong to the community were usually unbelievably crowded, and un-- and there was hardly any money or equipment to keep you going.
But I'm not sure how individual hospitals were existing.
Ghetto was all together still an existing community, which was still living, in spite of terrible conditions.
Normal life was continuing in every aspect.
There was, of course, government of the ghetto, which was the Judenrate.
There was-- there were some kind of-- there was some kind of education.
There was some kind of health service.
There was an-- there was a food supply, but food supply was, of course, something which was-- most of it was not official.
Most-- there was some official food supply because there were-- there were what was called kartki.
There were cards where you got some official rations.
There was official rationing.
But official rationing was, I think, just enough to die of hunger.
That was, you got some bread and some artificial honey or artificial-- artificial jam and some other things, but never enough to keep you alive.
So the food supply was almost-- most of it was relying on smuggling contraband through the walls and through the gates of the ghetto.
And that was in different shapes that started with little children, children starting from a very early age probably up to the age of-- up to the teenagers, and, of course, grown-ups.

The children were-- would just go under the wall through a hole in the wall, over the wall and go into the Polish part of Warsaw with some money and buy something, like buy a few pounds of potatoes, or bread or something else and try to smuggle themselves again through the ghetto in different ways.
Sometimes climbing over the wall, sometimes scrambling under the wall if there was a hole.
Sometimes, because there were gates and those gates were-- the gates were-- in the wall, there were places where you-- of passage between the-- between the Polish and-- part and the ghetto.
And that was called [NON-ENGLISH] because the German word is-- wache is guard.
They were guarded by-- there were-- by German, German gendarmerie, Polish police, which were so-called blue police, the navy blue police because of the uniform they called, and Jewish police.
And they all were guarding it, and the gate was, from time to time, being opened, and then they would allow whoever was allowed, whether it was a car or a cart or somebody to enter the ghetto.
And sometimes the gate was opened for a bit longer because something like a lorry would need to come.
And then sometimes the children would try to run through to rush the gate, and some of them would be shot.
Every time, some of them would be shot.
Some would get caught, and then they would have to empty their clothes.
And whatever they had in the way of supply, it would be confiscated, and they would be probably taken to prison or beaten up and so on.
It was-- and some would get through.
But many more would get over the wall or under the wall.
And there, of course, there was smuggling that was smuggling on the smaller scale, the children, individually.
There was smuggling then on a bigger scale and there were groups of smugglers--
--grown-ups which would arrange between-- to smuggle bigger amounts of stuff, like sacks of food, of flour, of some kind of cereal, of sugar to be thrown over the wall.
And there would be Jewish smugglers--
--who would collect it.
One must say here that there was a large underworld in Poland in Warsaw, and the underworld was-- there was some Jewish underworld as well.
And the Jewish underworld and the Polish underworld were--
--were cooperating.
They had good connection.
So as long as it was organized, as long as the Polish underworld would get paid, they were quite prepared to cooperate and to help in bringing those food supplies.
But there were some-- there were much bigger food supplies.
There were food supplies which were coming in lorries or in hole or in a huge horse-drawn carts.
And that was organized then with full knowledge of the gendarme on the [NON-ENGLISH], who would be paid, of course.
And then they would bribed.
Everybody was bribed.
Everybody was-- was-- knew about it very well.
And they would bring almost officially a whole-- whole wagons of supplies into the ghetto.
And there were of course people in the ghetto who were-- as for the social differences, there were the majority was poor, and a large amount was desperately poor.
But there were people who were well off.
There were people who were rich.

And there were people who were getting rich as a result of this trade and as a result of connections with the Germans because there-- there were-- there were-- or this were agents of the gestapo who were in the ghetto, who were practically eyes and ears of the Germans.

There were people-- everybody knew that there were people like Kohn and who were kind of ghetto barons who were very rich and were getting richer because they were all the time organizing the trade with the Germans, mainly of the food.
There was-- and so on and so forth.

So-- and there was a Pol-- Jewish police who were-- who graduated from relatively doing very-- graduated from cooperating with Germans on the very small scale into cooperating with them on quite a large scale.
They were-- and one has to say that those-- the Jewish police-- were all from best families.
They were young-- when they were formed in 1940, they were formed as so-called service of order, [NON-ENGLISH].
Help, you need to carry-- to-- to-- look after order in the ghetto.
And they were-- in the beginning, it was meant to be a wholly positive element to look that things-- that the law is being more or less kept, et cetera.
And they were to start-- many of them came from so-called good families.
Many of them were very nice young men.
They had-- in the beginning, their duties were not in any way horrible.
They had to do this and that, but-- and they had a uniform.
They didn't have any weapons.
They had-- the only weapon they had was a truncheon.
But and they had their hat, and they were usually dressed quite smartly.
They were putting-- they were dressing in-- they usually were wearing high leather boots and some kind of a fairly smart coat and a belt.
They looked quite smart.
And in the beginning, they had all kinds of duties, but they were not horrible.
But of course, quite soon after, they were being used for all kinds of duties which were much more doubtful, like they had, for example, to catch people for labor camps, for labor duties.
And some might have left and-- and not-- and not-- not gone along with it, but some have continued very well.
And the commanders of the Jewish police were cooperating more and more with the Germans, and were-- in the end became completely German agents.
So it was-- I would say that the whole of the Jewish police in the end, or most of it, were-- as far as their moral position is concerned, it was, I would say, very doubtful, very, very questionable.
And in the end, it was-- absolutely in the end when there was a liquidation, it was completely horrible.
Yes.

Well, the-- the hunger, particularly in this first winter, but even all the time after, I think, was something that was not even a day to day, but it was a most obvious occurrence so that one became terribly used to it in the end because people were hungry.
There were masses and masses of people who were hungry.
There were beggars everywhere, particularly in the main streets.
There were beggars sitting at the side of the pavement, the beggars sitting next to the gutter.
The beggars were-- in the main streets, there were many of them, and-- although I avoided those main streets because they were crowded, and they were horrible.
But I had to go to the main streets sometimes because my mother's family lived in Leszno, which was one that I went quite often to see them.
And they were living in Leszno, I think 71.
I can check it.
And that was one of the main streets, of course.
And you saw beggars everywhere, as I said, and dressed in rags.
And then the rags were getting worse and worse and torn and sometimes barefoot and so on.
And then they were dying as well.
The beggars were dying, and the poor people were dying, generally, and the poorer.
And if somebody died, if the family had no means of burying him, they would just take him out of the house and put him in the street.
And--
Lying covered with newspapers.
In the street.
In the street next to the wall of the house.
Those were, of course, the poorest who were-- who were dying and being buried in the street.
There were more people who were buried not in such a-- who were dying not in such an obvious fashion, who were dying maybe in their flats and taken out of there.
But there were-- but the Chevra Kadhisha, the burial service, was working all the time, and they were going with carts through the ghetto and collecting those--
--dead bodies who were lying at the side of the street.
And you saw them.
One-- one saw them in the main street, although I didn't look.
You saw just something lying there covered with a newspaper.
But sometimes I saw-- sometimes I remember I went somewhere and went in the small ghetto and then went back.
And there was this cart standing on the street, and there was a woman, a body lying there with the legs sticking out, and so on.
So that was-- so I couldn't avoid it.
I saw it.
And there were children, beggars, passing by and singing their songs and singing things like, [YIDDISH].
I remember that.
In Yiddish, of course.
They were usually s-- and although I didn't probably understand to start even what it meant, but I just-- [YIDDISH].
I remember that.
But they were singing all kinds of things.
And so death and-- and-- and the poor and the poverty and the terrible hunger was something.
I was not hungry myself.
I didn't go hungry.
I was not aware at this time-- of course, we didn't eat anything similar, even remotely, to what we ate before.
But we still had enough to eat.
But the hunger was everywhere.
The illness was everywhere.
The property was everywhere.
That was something which one was aware all the time.
And there were as well-- you couldn't-- couldn't bring food home very easily because there were what one called catchers.
They were teenagers, or maybe children, or maybe grown-ups who specialize in snatching, snatchers, if you want, who would snatch any packet out of your hands very quickly.
If they would see that you carry something, they would snatch it.
I don't think they would be interested in anything except food.
I think if it was not food, they probably would throw it or something.
But if it was food, they would immediately eat it up on the spot before-- even if they were beaten up.
Even if people would try to snatch it back from them, they would stuff it immediately into their mouth like bread or whatever it was.
That was something that was, of course, in the end, I think, my aunts and so on, they didn't want to bring anything almost to hide it and so on because one was also afraid of that.

But those people were literally dying of hunger and were-- were trying to snatch to get something to eat even though-- you know, even if it meant that they had to suffer a beating and being beaten or screamed at.

And of course, not-- again, there were those terrible contrasts everywhere because at the same time there was-- at the same time that there was the horrible poverty and hunger, there were as well people who ate very well.
And there were restaurants, and there were cafes.
Yes, yes, yes.
There were cafes.
There was even-- there was cultural life.
I think he's finishing now, no?
There was cultural life.
There were theaters.
There were academies.
There were-- academies, I mean, there-- that's a Polish word again.
That's something like certain official festivities, some ceremonies, and so on.
But there were theaters.
There were different kinds of theaters, although I wasn't to a theater myself, but I know that-- well I walked to one theater that I will mention to-- [NON-ENGLISH] theater.
But there were theaters were on bigger and smaller scale.
But there were as well restaurants.
There were as well-- there were as well coffee-- coffee houses, certainly places to eat.
And, in fact, there were places to eat and eat very well in ghetto, practically till the almost very end.
There were still at the very end.
But then, there were many restaurants.
And at the same time that people ate in the restaurant, there were people sitting next the restaurant and starving and-- and begging.
And there were those horrible contrasts.

And I think that the Germans were as well-- all too well-- all too well aware of that because that was later-- in 1942, in spring 1942, they were using the ghetto to turn films for anti-Semitic propaganda.
They were bringing the cameras into the ghetto and film crews and were turning proper bits of film, which we didn't know exactly what it meant.
And we didn't-- well, one didn't expect anything good.

But one didn't know what it meant, but it was obvious that they were turning them in order to make films showing how the Jews are terribly selfish and how they don't-- are not solidarity with each other and how they are immoral and how they are horrible.
They were turning all kinds of pictures where they were-- of the restaurants where there-- some people were eating, guzzling themselves and beggars dying of hunger next to it, precisely.
And pictures when they were herding men and women together and telling them to dance and maybe to undress and so on.
I'm not su-- I'm not sure.
I'm not 100% sure how we managed then to keep still relatively-- a relatively acceptable standard of living because, as I said, I'm not aware of being hungry.
It was-- life was, of course, not exactly happy.
It was-- one has-- it was suffering deprivations.
And one couldn't go out of the ghetto.
And one was only too aware of everything that was happening, but we were not hungry ourselves.
And we were not suffering a terrible deprivation ourselves.
I think it was we were partly helped because-- well, obviously, because we still had got some money from before, and we still had some things and were-- [COUGH].

And then because of the family of my mother, who were, as I said, they were-- they were very active and they had-- and two brothers of my mother, or even three brothers of my mother, were having those textile workshops, which and they must have had some access to food or to-- and-- and we still managed to exist in a relatively not deprived manner.
However, we were-- the flat where we lived in Chlodna-- I think 49 or 41, I'm not sure-- became then taken out of the whole house.

Of course, the whole group of houses was taken out of the ghetto, and that happened a number of times that the streets which were on the-- almost on the outskirts of the ghetto near the wall, where suddenly the Germans decreed that the ghetto has to be reduced in size.
And a few streets or a groups of houses were taken out.

So they suddenly decided that this part of the Chlodna was taken out, and we had to move out of there very quickly, and we still managed to get some of our things and most of our things, I think, and furniture that we had there into some other places.
But we had for a while no place to live.
Thank you.
It's the end of tape number four.
Four?
Is it four?
